title: MBLEx D2 Kinesiology v1 (best-answer reasoning, prompt-locked)

questions.prompt: During a bodyweight squat, movement at the hips and knees occurs primarily in which plane?
questions.answer: Sagittal plane
questions.why: Squats are mainly flexion and extension, which are sagittal-plane actions.
questions.trap: Frontal and transverse planes mostly stabilize, not drive the main movement here.

questions.prompt: In a jumping jack, shoulder and hip abduction and adduction occur primarily in which plane?
questions.answer: Frontal plane
questions.why: Abduction and adduction are frontal-plane motions around an anterior-posterior axis.
questions.trap: Transverse rotation can occur, but it is not the dominant action.

questions.prompt: A client turns to look behind while backing a car. This trunk action is primarily:
questions.answer: Rotation in the transverse plane
questions.why: Looking behind mainly requires axial trunk rotation in the transverse plane.
questions.trap: Extension may accompany turning, but rotation is the key movement.

questions.prompt: Which muscle team best supports scapular upward rotation during pain-free overhead reach?
questions.answer: Serratus anterior and lower trapezius
questions.why: Serratus anterior and trapezius force-couple to produce efficient upward rotation.
questions.trap: Downward rotators may feel active but often oppose full overhead mechanics.

questions.prompt: A positive Trendelenburg sign during single-leg stance most strongly suggests weakness of the:
questions.answer: Stance-side gluteus medius
questions.why: Contralateral pelvic drop reflects insufficient hip abductor support on the stance side.
questions.trap: Distal ankle muscles affect foot control, not primary pelvic leveling.

questions.prompt: For toe clearance during normal swing phase, which ankle action is most required?
questions.answer: Dorsiflexion
questions.why: Dorsiflexion lifts the forefoot and reduces toe drag during swing.
questions.trap: Plantarflexion is more important for push-off, not swing clearance.

questions.prompt: At terminal knee extension in open chain, the primary antagonist to quadriceps action is the:
questions.answer: Hamstring group
questions.why: Hamstrings flex the knee, directly opposing quadriceps-driven extension.
questions.trap: Popliteus helps unlocking, but not as the main antagonist through extension.

questions.prompt: A client with rounded shoulders and scapular protraction most commonly presents with shortened:
questions.answer: Pectoralis minor
questions.why: Short pectoralis minor can pull the scapula into protraction and anterior tilt.
questions.trap: Rhomboids are often lengthened or inhibited in this posture pattern.
questions.hook: Rounded shoulders often mean pec minor.

questions.prompt: When anterior pelvic tilt is linked to short hip flexors, the BEST first movement strategy is:

choices.A: Hip flexor lengthening with glute activation

questions.answer: Hip flexor lengthening with glute activation
questions.why: This pairing reduces front-side stiffness and restores posterior pelvic control.
questions.trap: Lumbar extensor-only focus can reinforce the same extension-biased posture.

questions.prompt: A clear foot slap immediately after heel strike most strongly suggests weakness in the:
questions.answer: Tibialis anterior
questions.why: Tibialis anterior eccentrically controls plantarflexion after initial contact.
questions.trap: Soleus weakness affects push-off more than controlled forefoot lowering.

questions.prompt: Which primary action is most associated with gluteus maximus during functional movement?
questions.answer: Hip extension
questions.why: Gluteus maximus is a major hip extensor during rising and acceleration tasks.
questions.trap: Knee extension is primarily a quadriceps role, not glute max.

questions.prompt: During early stance, normal foot pronation is MOST useful for:
questions.answer: Shock absorption and surface adaptation
questions.why: Controlled pronation dissipates load and adapts the foot to terrain.
questions.trap: A rigid midfoot is more relevant near push-off, not early loading.

questions.prompt: A client has shoulder pain after 90 degrees of abduction and visibly limited scapular upward rotation. BEST exercise focus?

choices.A: Serratus anterior and lower trapezius retraining
choices.D: Complete overhead rest for six weeks

questions.answer: Serratus anterior and lower trapezius retraining
questions.why: This best restores scapular mechanics for elevation. Thoracic or cuff work helps, but usually cannot fix the upward-rotation deficit alone.
questions.trap: Rest can reduce symptoms briefly but does not rebuild overhead movement strategy.

questions.prompt: An overhead lifter reports painful arc symptoms but full strength and no trauma signs. What is the BEST next approach?

choices.A: Immediately max-load overhead to test tolerance
choices.B: Temporarily reduce overhead volume and retrain scapular posterior tilt
choices.D: Treat only with passive pectoral stretching

questions.answer: Temporarily reduce overhead volume and retrain scapular posterior tilt
questions.why: Load modification plus motor retraining addresses symptoms and mechanics. Stretching alone may feel better, but often misses the key movement fault.
questions.trap: Long complete shutdown can decondition tissue without teaching better mechanics.

questions.prompt: During squats, both knees collapse inward despite adequate ankle mobility. Which intervention is BEST first?

choices.C: Strengthen hip abductors and external rotators with knee-tracking cues

questions.answer: Strengthen hip abductors and external rotators with knee-tracking cues
questions.why: This targets frontal-plane control deficits directly. Stretching can help comfort, but usually does not normalize dynamic valgus on its own.
questions.trap: Removing squats entirely removes a useful pattern for graded retraining.

questions.prompt: In gait, contralateral pelvic drop appears in mid-stance with no pain. BEST biomechanical interpretation?
questions.answer: Insufficient stance-side hip abductor support
questions.why: Mid-stance pelvic drop classically reflects stance-side abductor control deficit. Plantarflexor weakness can alter timing but does not directly explain this frontal-plane sign.
questions.trap: Hamstring length alone is not the main pelvic level controller.

questions.prompt: A runner demonstrates early heel rise and persistent pronation. Ankle dorsiflexion is limited. BEST corrective priority?

choices.A: Improve calf length and dorsiflexion mobility

questions.answer: Improve calf length and dorsiflexion mobility
questions.why: Restoring dorsiflexion addresses the ankle rocker deficit driving both compensations. Hamstring stretching may help comfort, but misses the key restriction.
questions.trap: Permanent heel elevation masks limitation without restoring motion capacity.

questions.prompt: A client sits with posterior pelvic tilt and flattened lumbar curve. Hamstrings test short. BEST program emphasis?

choices.B: Improve hamstring length and lumbar extension endurance

questions.answer: Improve hamstring length and lumbar extension endurance
questions.why: This addresses both posterior chain tension bias and extensor endurance. Abdominal work helps trunk control, but alone may reinforce flexion-dominant posture.
questions.trap: Permanent flexion avoidance reduces function without building adaptable control.

questions.prompt: For upper-crossed posture with forward head and scapular depression, what is the BEST starting strategy?

choices.A: Strengthen upper trapezius and stretch lower trapezius
choices.C: Strengthen deep neck flexors and lower trap while lengthening pectoralis minor

questions.answer: Strengthen deep neck flexors and lower trap while lengthening pectoralis minor
questions.why: This targets common weak and short contributors together. Passive treatment may help temporarily, but active rebalancing is needed for carryover.
questions.trap: Cervical extension-only work can worsen anterior head translation strategies.

questions.prompt: A client with impingement signs shows scapular anterior tilt during elevation. Which plan is BEST?

choices.B: Complete overhead avoidance for three months
choices.D: Load modification plus serratus and lower trap strengthening with pec minor mobility

questions.answer: Load modification plus serratus and lower trap strengthening with pec minor mobility
questions.why: This combines symptom modulation and movement correction. Thoracic mobility helps, but isolated mobility usually fails to normalize loaded scapular control.
questions.trap: Deltoid-heavy loading can aggravate symptoms if scapular mechanics stay faulty.

questions.prompt: Lateral elbow pain worsens with gripping and wrist extension. BEST early exercise approach?

choices.A: Progressive wrist extensor loading with temporary volume reduction

questions.answer: Progressive wrist extensor loading with temporary volume reduction
questions.why: Progressive loading builds tendon capacity while reducing overload. Immobilization may calm pain, but prolonged unloading delays functional adaptation.
questions.trap: Friction alone does not rebuild load tolerance for gripping demands.

questions.prompt: Posterior thigh tension appears during straight-leg raise, but slump is negative and ankle dorsiflexion does not change symptoms. BEST interpretation?
questions.answer: Primary hamstring extensibility limitation
questions.why: When neural sensitizers do not alter symptoms, muscular length limitation is more likely. Neural involvement remains possible, but this pattern favors hamstrings.
questions.trap: Symptom location alone can overcall neural mechanisms.

questions.prompt: A client cannot generate strong elbow flexion when the shoulder is already fully flexed overhead. BEST explanation?

choices.C: Active insufficiency of the biceps brachii

questions.answer: Active insufficiency of the biceps brachii
questions.why: A two-joint muscle shortened at both joints loses force capacity. Triceps tension can influence motion, but this classic pattern indicates biceps active insufficiency.
questions.trap: Distal fatigue is less relevant than shoulder-elbow position context.

questions.prompt: For early patellofemoral pain rehab during stairs, which option is generally BEST first?

choices.A: Open-chain knee extension to terminal lockout only
choices.D: Controlled closed-chain step-down with hip-knee alignment cues

questions.answer: Controlled closed-chain step-down with hip-knee alignment cues
questions.why: This builds relevant functional control under manageable load. Open-chain work can help later, but is often less transferable early.
questions.trap: Jumping to deep high-load lunges can exceed current tolerance.

questions.prompt: A client stands in knee hyperextension during quiet posture. BEST movement priority?

choices.A: Cue slight knee flexion and train hamstring plus proprioceptive control
choices.C: Brace in full extension all day

questions.answer: Cue slight knee flexion and train hamstring plus proprioceptive control
questions.why: This changes motor habit and improves posterior knee support. Bracing in lockout may feel stable, but can preserve the same faulty strategy.
questions.trap: Single-muscle strengthening alone rarely changes persistent postural patterns.

questions.prompt: A client walks with bilateral toe-out but no pain. What is the BEST first kinesiology step?

choices.B: Assess hip rotation ROM and lower-limb alignment before retraining

questions.answer: Assess hip rotation ROM and lower-limb alignment before retraining
questions.why: Assessment should identify structural and mobility contributors before cue changes. Immediate correction cues may create compensations when root causes are unknown.
questions.trap: Orthotics may help selected cases but should not replace movement assessment.

questions.prompt: In single-leg squat, the pelvis drops and trunk shifts away from stance leg. BEST corrective emphasis?

choices.C: Stance-side hip abductor strength and lateral chain control

questions.answer: Stance-side hip abductor strength and lateral chain control
questions.why: This directly targets frontal-plane pelvic and trunk control deficits. Passive stretching may ease tension but rarely restores single-leg stability alone.
questions.trap: Avoiding unilateral tasks removes the context needed for retraining.

questions.prompt: A client has flexion-intolerant low back pain during lifting. Which coaching strategy is BEST?

choices.D: Teach hip hinge with neutral spine and graded load

questions.answer: Teach hip hinge with neutral spine and graded load
questions.why: Hip hinge retraining redistributes load while maintaining function. Bracing helps pressure control, but without pattern change it often fails under real lifting tasks.
questions.trap: Permanent lifting avoidance reduces resilience and daily capacity.

questions.prompt: To bias gastrocnemius length testing, which setup is BEST?

choices.A: Measure ankle dorsiflexion with knee extended
choices.B: Measure ankle dorsiflexion with knee flexed deeply
choices.C: Measure hip flexion with knee bent
choices.D: Measure knee extension in seated posture

questions.answer: Measure ankle dorsiflexion with knee extended
questions.why: Knee extension tensions gastrocnemius during dorsiflexion testing. Knee-flexed dorsiflexion is useful, but biases soleus more than gastrocnemius.
questions.trap: Hip or seated knee tests do not isolate ankle plantarflexor length well.

questions.prompt: To bias soleus length testing, which setup is BEST?
questions.answer: Ankle dorsiflexion with knee flexed
questions.why: Flexing the knee slackens gastrocnemius so dorsiflexion limits better reflect soleus contribution. Knee-extended testing remains more gastrocnemius-influenced.
questions.trap: Hip and knee-only tests cannot answer this ankle-specific length question.

questions.prompt: A field athlete with recent adductor strain wants to sprint tomorrow. BEST return-to-speed criterion?

choices.C: Pain-free resisted adduction and controlled lateral lunge
choices.D: No tenderness with static palpation only

questions.answer: Pain-free resisted adduction and controlled lateral lunge
questions.why: This includes strength and movement-demand checks relevant to sprinting. Passive ROM is useful, but does not confirm dynamic tissue readiness.
questions.trap: Palpation alone can miss high-speed load deficits.

questions.prompt: Runner with lateral knee pain reports temporary relief from foam rolling only. BEST next step?

choices.B: Stop running completely for two months
choices.D: Progress hip abductor strength and cadence-based gait adjustment

questions.answer: Progress hip abductor strength and cadence-based gait adjustment
questions.why: This addresses likely load drivers, not only symptoms. Stretching may feel better briefly, but rarely changes recurrent running mechanics alone.
questions.trap: Long full rest can reduce fitness without solving the root movement issue.

questions.prompt: A keyboard worker reports nighttime thumb-index-middle numbness relieved by shaking the hand. BEST kinesiology interpretation?

choices.A: Median nerve compression at the carpal tunnel is likely
choices.B: Upper trapezius trigger points alone explain this pattern
choices.C: Pronator syndrome is most likely despite nighttime pattern
choices.D: Primary elbow extensor tendinopathy explains nocturnal numbness

questions.answer: Median nerve compression at the carpal tunnel is likely
questions.why: Digit distribution and nighttime provocation strongly fit carpal tunnel. Pronator involvement can occur, but this timing pattern favors wrist-level median compression.
questions.trap: Myofascial pain can coexist but usually does not create this specific nerve pattern alone.

questions.prompt: During wall push-up, the medial border of the scapula wings prominently. Which muscle is MOST implicated?
questions.answer: Serratus anterior
questions.why: Serratus anterior insufficiency commonly causes medial border winging during pushing tasks. Rhomboid issues affect retraction, but this sign points more to serratus control.
questions.trap: Teres minor is a rotator cuff muscle, not the primary scapular protraction stabilizer.

questions.prompt: A client with knee osteoarthritis shows varus loading during gait. BEST exercise emphasis?

choices.A: Only seated knee extension with heavy load
choices.C: Hip abductor strengthening with controlled step tasks

questions.answer: Hip abductor strengthening with controlled step tasks
questions.why: This can improve frontal-plane control and reduce medial knee load strategy. Quadriceps work helps strength, but alone may not alter varus movement behavior.
questions.trap: Stopping all walking may worsen conditioning and confidence.

questions.prompt: For anterior pelvic tilt with excessive lumbar lordosis, which combination is BEST?

choices.A: Strengthen lumbar extensors and hip flexors
choices.B: Stretch glutes and strengthen hip flexors
choices.D: Lengthen hip flexors and strengthen glutes with abdominals

questions.answer: Lengthen hip flexors and strengthen glutes with abdominals
questions.why: This addresses common short and weak contributors together. Bracing drills can aid awareness, but alone often fail to change loaded pelvic control.
questions.trap: More extensor dominance can reinforce the same extension-biased posture.

questions.prompt: Desk worker with thoracic kyphosis fatigues quickly in upright sitting. BEST training focus?

choices.A: Thoracic extension mobility and scapular endurance work

questions.answer: Thoracic extension mobility and scapular endurance work
questions.why: This targets the segment and endurance demands of sustained upright posture. Neck-only work can help symptoms, but misses thoracic extension capacity.
questions.trap: Avoidance delays adaptation to needed daily posture tolerance.

questions.prompt: A distance runner overstrides with recurrent shin pain. Which gait cue is BEST supported?

choices.A: Land farther in front for braking control
choices.B: Increase cadence modestly to reduce overstride
choices.C: Use maximal vertical bounce for propulsion
choices.D: Keep cadence unchanged and increase stride length

questions.answer: Increase cadence modestly to reduce overstride
questions.why: A modest cadence increase often reduces braking load and tibial stress exposure. Footwear changes may help comfort but less directly change overstride mechanics.
questions.trap: Longer stride usually increases braking and symptom-driving loads.

questions.prompt: A baseball pitcher has late-cocking shoulder pain, reduced internal rotation, and visible scapular dyskinesis. BEST integrated plan?

choices.A: Immediate full-intensity throwing with posterior shoulder stretching only
choices.B: Complete throwing shutdown for three months without retraining
choices.C: Gradual throwing-load reduction plus posterior shoulder mobility and scapular control training
choices.D: Only passive pectoral release before games

questions.answer: Gradual throwing-load reduction plus posterior shoulder mobility and scapular control training
questions.why: This combines irritability management, mobility restoration, and control retraining. Full shutdown may calm symptoms, but without graded reloading and mechanics work, durable return is less likely.
questions.trap: Single-modality soft tissue work rarely resolves a multi-factor throwing problem.

questions.prompt: During gait analysis, a client leans trunk over the stance leg and reduces opposite pelvic drop. BEST biomechanical interpretation?

choices.A: Compensation for weak opposite quadratus lumborum
choices.C: Compensation for weak dorsiflexors on swing side
choices.D: Compensation to reduce demand on stance-side hip abductors

questions.answer: Compensation to reduce demand on stance-side hip abductors
questions.why: This is a classic strategy to shorten the hip abductor moment arm demand. Other findings may coexist, but this trunk shift primarily unloads stance-side abductors.
questions.trap: Distal findings can distract from this proximal frontal-plane compensation.

questions.prompt: A cyclist develops ring and little finger paresthesia after long elbow-flexed rides, with medial elbow sensitivity. BEST kinesiology interpretation?

choices.A: Ulnar nerve irritation at the cubital tunnel is most likely
choices.B: Median nerve compression at carpal tunnel is most likely
choices.C: Primary rhomboid referral explains distal paresthesia pattern
choices.D: Primary Achilles overuse explains hand symptoms

questions.answer: Ulnar nerve irritation at the cubital tunnel is most likely
questions.why: Symptom distribution plus prolonged elbow flexion strongly fits cubital tunnel involvement. Median compression is common, but usually affects a different digit pattern.
questions.trap: Referral pain can coexist, but this neurologic pattern is more specific.

questions.prompt: A swayback pattern shows posterior pelvic shift, thoracic kyphosis, and knee hyperextension. BEST first corrective emphasis?

choices.A: Strengthen hamstrings and stretch hip flexors only
choices.B: Restore stacked alignment with hip-flexor and deep-core control plus hamstring length management
choices.D: Avoid corrective exercise until symptoms disappear

questions.answer: Restore stacked alignment with hip-flexor and deep-core control plus hamstring length management
questions.why: This addresses global strategy and key control deficits, not one tissue. Hamstring-only emphasis can further reinforce posterior pelvic dominance in swayback.
questions.trap: Aggressive extension work may increase compensatory hinging rather than stacked posture.

questions.prompt: A client has painful shoulder abduction with visible scapular downward rotation at rest. Which intervention is BEST priority?

choices.A: Heavy latissimus strengthening for shoulder support
choices.C: Upward-rotation motor retraining with serratus and lower trapezius loading

questions.answer: Upward-rotation motor retraining with serratus and lower trapezius loading
questions.why: This directly addresses the scapular force-couple problem driving painful elevation. Joint stretching may help range, but often fails without scapular control retraining.
questions.trap: Downward-rotator strengthening can worsen the resting scapular bias.

questions.prompt: Runner shows knee valgus, hip internal rotation, and limited ankle dorsiflexion during step-down. BEST initial sequencing?

choices.A: Train knee alignment only without mobility work
choices.B: Only stretch hamstrings and continue full mileage
choices.C: Only hip strengthening without movement retraining
choices.D: Restore dorsiflexion, then integrate hip control in task-specific retraining

questions.answer: Restore dorsiflexion, then integrate hip control in task-specific retraining
questions.why: This handles both the distal mobility limiter and proximal control need. Hip strengthening helps, but remains incomplete when ankle restriction keeps forcing compensation.
questions.trap: Alignment cues alone often fail if mobility cannot support the target position.

questions.prompt: A painter gets hand numbness during sustained overhead work; symptoms ease after lowering the arm. BEST kinesiology action plan?

choices.A: Reduce overhead duration and retrain scapular-thoracic mechanics with graded exposure
choices.B: Maximize overhead time daily to desensitize quickly

questions.answer: Reduce overhead duration and retrain scapular-thoracic mechanics with graded exposure
questions.why: This reduces provocation while building capacity and mechanics progressively. Full inactivity may reduce symptoms temporarily but does not prepare for occupational overhead demand.
questions.trap: Rapid exposure increases can flare neural irritability and guarding.

questions.prompt: A client with insertional Achilles pain worsens after deep heel-drop stretching. BEST loading strategy now?

choices.B: Use progressive calf raises on flat ground while avoiding deep dorsiflexion early
choices.C: Stop all calf loading for three months

questions.answer: Use progressive calf raises on flat ground while avoiding deep dorsiflexion early
questions.why: This builds tendon capacity while limiting insertional compression aggravation. Full unloading may calm pain, but often reduces tendon tolerance and function over time.
questions.trap: Deeper stretch is not always better when compression sensitivity dominates.

questions.prompt: Client with anterior hip pain shows symptoms during deep flexion, adduction, and internal rotation. BEST exercise emphasis?

choices.A: Deep loaded squats to tolerance every session
choices.C: Strengthen gluteal control while limiting provocative deep flexion-adduction ranges

questions.answer: Strengthen gluteal control while limiting provocative deep flexion-adduction ranges
questions.why: This protects irritated positions while improving control in usable ranges. Passive stretching can feel helpful, but alone rarely changes dynamic loading behavior.
questions.trap: Repeated provocation often sustains irritability and delays progression.

questions.prompt: Six weeks after ACL reconstruction, a client still lacks 5 degrees of knee extension and has quad lag. BEST rehab priority?

choices.A: Begin plyometrics immediately to normalize gait
choices.C: Prioritize high-load leg press through partial flexion
choices.D: Restore full extension and quadriceps activation before higher-level loading

questions.answer: Restore full extension and quadriceps activation before higher-level loading
questions.why: These are foundational deficits that drive compensations if ignored. Global strengthening helps, but progression quality remains limited until extension and quad activation normalize.
questions.trap: Early plyometrics can amplify faulty mechanics and irritability.

questions.prompt: Posterior hip pain radiates down the thigh when sitting, but lumbar repeated motions are clear and symptoms worsen with prolonged hip external rotation. BEST interpretation?

choices.A: Deep gluteal or piriformis-related neural irritation is more likely
choices.B: Primary lumbar discogenic pain is most likely
choices.D: Primary plantar fascia overload explains symptoms

questions.answer: Deep gluteal or piriformis-related neural irritation is more likely
questions.why: Hip-position sensitivity with clear lumbar repeated motions favors extra-spinal neural irritation. Lumbar referral remains a safety consideration, but current behavior points elsewhere.
questions.trap: Symptom location alone can mislead without movement-provocation context.

questions.prompt: In modified Thomas testing, the thigh stays elevated and the knee extends from 90 to 40 degrees while supine. BEST conclusion?

choices.B: Rectus femoris shortness contributes to the hip-flexor limitation

questions.answer: Rectus femoris shortness contributes to the hip-flexor limitation
questions.why: Knee extension drift during modified Thomas indicates two-joint rectus femoris tension. Iliopsoas may also contribute, but the knee change specifically implicates rectus involvement.
questions.trap: Hamstring length is assessed differently and does not explain this pattern.
